questions.question: Explain how the trapezoidal rule can be implemented using CUDA. Describe the role of each GPU thread and how results are combined.
keyPoints: Each CUDA thread computes f(x_i) for its assigned subinterval point using its global thread index
Partial results are stored in shared memory within each block
Parallel reduction within blocks combines partial sums using a tree-based approach
Block-level results are combined on the host or via atomicAdd to get the final integral

questions.question: Compare the CUDA and MPI approaches to parallelizing the trapezoidal rule. Discuss advantages and disadvantages of each.
keyPoints: CUDA: Thousands of lightweight GPU threads evaluate f(x) simultaneously with fine-grained parallelism
MPI: Fewer processes with coarse-grained parallelism, each handling a contiguous interval chunk
CUDA advantage: Massive parallelism, no inter-process communication overhead, good for embarrassingly parallel problems
MPI advantage: Works on distributed memory systems, can handle larger problems across multiple nodes
CUDA limitation: Data must be transferred between host and device, limited by GPU memory
MPI limitation: Communication overhead for Bcast and Reduce operations, limited by network bandwidth

questions.question: Describe the parallel reduction technique used to sum partial integrals in a CUDA implementation.
keyPoints: Threads store partial sums in shared memory array
In each reduction step, half the active threads add their neighbor's value
Number of active threads halves at each step (stride doubles)
After log2(blockDim) steps, thread 0 holds the block's total sum
Block sums are written to global memory and combined on the host

questions.question: Write the CUDA kernel function signature and thread-to-subinterval mapping for the trapezoidal rule. Explain how boundary conditions are handled.
keyPoints: Kernel signature: __global__ void trapezoidalKernel(double a, double h, int n, double* result)
Global index: int i = threadIdx.x + blockIdx.x * blockDim.x
Point mapping: x_i = a + i * h
Boundary condition: First point f(a) and last point f(b) are weighted by 0.5
Interior points get weight 1.0 in the trapezoidal formula
Guard condition: if (i < n) to handle cases where total threads exceed n
